feedback.text: Hello, I support the Digital Fairness Act, but I believe there could be some worthwhile additions that could benefit all EU citizens. For example, there has been a worrying trend for more than a decade now in the video game industry that lets publishers and developers make their products, i.e. video games, permanently unusable after they stop supporting them. This is happening even though these products are marketed and sold as "goods," whereas the aforementioned practice of leaving video games in a dysfunctional state after support for them ends sounds more like a "service." There is clearly some ambiguity here in relation to digital fairness, hence why I personally think that it would be prudent that the Digital Fairness Act considers this issue, which is also currently gaining traction in the form of the European Citizens' Initiative "Stop Destroying Videogames". On a more personal note, I am one of those people that has grown up with video games of all kinds, and it saddens me that there are titles that will not be able to be archived, preserved or even just experienced for a second time. Of course, the question whether video games are art is still an open one, but I nonetheless believe that it's unnecessary to destroy something that has the potential to leave someone with a positive impression. Regards, Daniel Iliev
